question: What is Git? What is the difference between Git and GitHub?
idealAnswer: Git is a distributed version control system (VCS) that tracks changes to source code during software development. Created by Linus Torvalds in 2005. Key feature: every developer has a complete copy of the repository history. GitHub is a web-based hosting platform for Git repositories. It adds features like: pull requests, issue tracking, CI/CD (Actions), code review, project management. Git is the tool; GitHub is a service that hosts Git repos. Alternatives to GitHub: GitLab, Bitbucket, Azure DevOps. Git works locally without any hosting service.
keyPoints: Git is VCS, GitHub is hosting platform

question: Explain Git branching. What are the common branching strategies?
idealAnswer: A branch is an independent line of development. It allows you to work on features without affecting the main code. git branch feature-name creates a branch. git checkout feature-name or git switch feature-name switches to it. Common strategies: 1) Git Flow: main (production), develop (integration), feature/*, release/*, hotfix/*. Good for scheduled releases. 2) GitHub Flow: main + feature branches + pull requests. Simple. Good for continuous deployment. 3) Trunk-based: Everyone commits to main/trunk frequently. Short-lived feature branches (1-2 days max). Good for experienced teams. Best practices: Keep branches short-lived. Use descriptive names (feature/user-auth, fix/login-bug). Always create branches from an updated main. Delete branches after merging.
keyPoints: Git Flow vs GitHub Flow vs trunk-based

question: What is the difference between git merge and git rebase? When would you use each?
idealAnswer: git merge: Combines two branches by creating a new merge commit. Preserves the complete history (you can see when branches were created and merged). Creates a non-linear history (merge diamonds). Command: git checkout main && git merge feature. git rebase: Moves/replays your branch's commits on top of another branch. Creates a linear history (as if you wrote all commits sequentially). Rewrites commit hashes. Command: git checkout feature && git rebase main. Use merge when: Working on shared branches, you want to preserve branch history, collaborating with others. Use rebase when: Cleaning up local commits before pushing, you want a clean linear history, working on a personal feature branch. Golden rule: NEVER rebase commits that have been pushed and shared with others.
keyPoints: merge creates merge commit preserves history
rebase replays commits creates linear history

question: What is a merge conflict in Git? How do you resolve it?
idealAnswer:
A merge conflict occurs when two branches modify the same part of the same file and Git can't automatically determine which version to keep. How to resolve: 1) Git marks the conflicted file with conflict markers:
```
<<<<<<< HEAD
your changes
=======
their changes
>>>>>>> feature-branch
```
2) Open the file and manually edit — keep the desired code, remove the markers. 3) Stage the resolved file: git add filename. 4) Complete the merge: git commit. Prevention tips: Pull frequently, communicate with team, keep changes small, avoid editing same files. Tools: VS Code has a built-in merge editor. git mergetool launches a graphical tool. git diff shows differences.
keyPoints: same part of same file modified

question: Explain the staging area (index) in Git. What is the difference between git add, git commit, and git push?
idealAnswer: Git has three areas: Working Directory (your files), Staging Area/Index (prepared for next commit), Repository (committed history). git add: Moves changes from working directory to staging area. Selects what goes into the next commit. git add file.txt (specific file), git add . (all changes). git commit: Saves staged changes to the local repository as a snapshot. Creates a commit with message. git commit -m 'message'. git push: Uploads local commits to a remote repository (GitHub). git push origin main. Flow: Edit files → git add (stage) → git commit (save locally) → git push (share remotely). Think of it as: add = put in shopping cart, commit = purchase, push = ship to warehouse.
keyPoints: add stages, commit saves, push shares
staging area selects what to commit

question: What is the difference between git pull and git fetch? When would you use each?
idealAnswer: git fetch: Downloads changes from the remote repository but does NOT merge them into your local branch. Updates remote-tracking branches (origin/main). Safe — your working code is untouched. You can review changes before merging: git log origin/main. git pull: Does git fetch + git merge in one step. Downloads AND merges remote changes into your current branch. Convenient but can cause unexpected merge conflicts. Equivalent to: git fetch origin && git merge origin/main. Use fetch when: You want to review changes before merging, working on important code, want more control. Use pull when: You trust the remote changes, doing quick updates, working alone. Best practice: Many teams prefer git fetch + git merge (or rebase) for more control over what gets merged.
keyPoints: fetch downloads only, pull downloads + merges

question: How do you undo changes in Git? Explain git reset, git revert, and git checkout for undoing.
idealAnswer: git checkout -- file: Discards changes in working directory (unstaged changes). Restores file to last committed version. Dangerous — changes are lost. git reset: Moves HEAD pointer. Three modes: --soft (uncommit, keep staged), --mixed (uncommit, unstage, keep in working dir — default), --hard (uncommit and delete all changes — DANGEROUS). git reset HEAD~1 undoes last commit. git revert: Creates a NEW commit that undoes a previous commit. Safe for shared/pushed commits because it doesn't rewrite history. git revert abc123 creates a new commit that undoes commit abc123. Rule: Use revert for pushed commits (public history). Use reset for local unpushed commits. Use checkout for working directory changes. git stash: Temporarily shelves changes (like a clipboard).
keyPoints: revert for public, reset for local

question: What is a Pull Request (PR)? Why are they used in software development?
idealAnswer: A Pull Request is a request to merge code from one branch into another (usually feature branch → main). It's a GitHub/GitLab concept (not a Git feature). Process: 1) Create a feature branch and make changes. 2) Push the branch to remote. 3) Open a PR on GitHub — describe what you changed and why. 4) Team members review the code — suggest changes, ask questions. 5) CI/CD runs automated tests. 6) After approval, the PR is merged into main. Why PRs are important: Code review — catches bugs before they reach production. Knowledge sharing — team learns about changes. Quality gate — ensures tests pass. Documentation — PR description explains the 'why'. Discussion — team can discuss approach before merging. Best practices: Small PRs (easier to review), descriptive titles, link to issues, add screenshots for UI changes.

question: What is git stash? When would you use it?
idealAnswer: git stash temporarily saves uncommitted changes (both staged and unstaged) without committing them. Your working directory becomes clean (matches last commit). Commands: git stash — save current changes. git stash pop — restore most recent stash and remove from stash list. git stash apply — restore but keep in stash list. git stash list — show all stashes. git stash drop — delete a stash. git stash clear — delete all stashes. Use cases: 1) Need to switch branches but have uncommitted work. 2) Need to pull changes but have local modifications. 3) Want to test something on a clean working directory. 4) Temporarily park work to handle an urgent bug fix. Example: Working on feature, urgent bug comes in → git stash, switch to main, fix bug, switch back, git stash pop.
keyPoints: use when switching branches with dirty work

question: What is a .gitignore file? Give examples of what you would typically include in it.
idealAnswer:
.gitignore tells Git which files/directories to ignore (not track). Files listed here won't appear in git status, won't be staged, and won't be committed. Placed in the repo root. Common entries:
```
# Dependencies
node_modules/
vendor/

# Build output
dist/
build/
bin/
obj/

# Environment / secrets
.env
.env.local
appsettings.Development.json

# IDE files
.idea/
.vscode/
*.swp

# OS files
.DS_Store
Thumbs.db

# Compiled files
*.o
*.class
*.pyc
```
Rules: * matches multiple characters, / at end means directory, ! negates (include a previously ignored file), # for comments. If a file is already tracked, adding it to .gitignore won't untrack it — use git rm --cached file first.
keyPoints: already tracked files need git rm --cached

question: What is git cherry-pick? When would you use it?
idealAnswer: git cherry-pick applies a specific commit from one branch to another — it copies a single commit without merging the entire branch. Command: git cherry-pick <commit-hash>. It creates a new commit on the current branch with the same changes (but a different hash). Use cases: 1) A bug fix was committed to the wrong branch — cherry-pick it to the correct one. 2) You need one specific feature from a branch that isn't ready to merge. 3) Backporting: applying a fix from main to an older release branch. 4) Selectively moving commits during a messy branch restructuring. Caution: Cherry-picking can create duplicate commits (same changes, different hashes). If the picked commit depends on earlier commits, it may cause conflicts. Don't use it as a substitute for proper merge/rebase workflows.
keyPoints: copies specific commit to current branch
creates new commit with same changes
use for bug fix on wrong branch

question: Show the sequence of Git commands to: create a new repo, add files, make a commit, create a branch, and push to GitHub.
idealAnswer:
```bash
# Initialize new repo
git init

# Add files
echo '# My Project' > README.md
git add .

# First commit
git commit -m 'Initial commit'

# Create and switch to new branch
git checkout -b feature/user-auth
# Or: git branch feature/user-auth && git checkout feature/user-auth

# Make changes and commit
echo 'new code' > auth.js
git add auth.js
git commit -m 'Add user authentication'

# Add remote
git remote add origin https://github.com/user/repo.git

# Push main branch
git checkout main
git push -u origin main

# Push feature branch
git checkout feature/user-auth
git push -u origin feature/user-auth
```
-u (--set-upstream) links local branch to remote for future push/pull.
